title-group.article-title: Heightened propofol sensitivity and distinct electroencephalographic signatures in patients with valvular heart disease

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/03t1yn780grid.412679.f0000 0004 1771 3402Department of Anesthesiology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Anhui Medical University, Hefei, 230022 China

abstract.title: Highlights
abstract.p: Valvular heart disease (VD) patients exhibited 34% lower propofol CeLOC (2.8 vs. 4.25 µg/mL; p < 0.0001) versus controls, persisting through intubation.

VD patients showed elevated β and θ power (β: 3.16 vs. 1.68, p = 0.0303; θ: 1.21 vs. 0.88, p = 0.0166), higher spectral power ratio (0.11 vs. 0.32; p < 0.0001), and impaired β and δ bicoherence (β: 40.95 vs. 34.75, p = 0.0253; δ:43.5 vs. 30.95, p < 0.0001) during unconsciousness.
Burst suppression was intensified in VD (ratio: 0.32 vs. 0.11; p < 0.0001), with longer suppression epochs (1.23 vs. 2.09, p < 0.0001) and unstable state transitions (20.75 vs. 48.75, p < 0.0001).
Disease-specific EEG biomarkers (SPR, bicoherence, burst suppression) enable precision anesthesia protocols for cardiac populations, addressing conventional neuromonitoring limitations.
Heightened propofol sensitivity and distinct electroencephalographic signatures in patients with valvular heart disease.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The significant association between anesthetic sensitivity and postoperative outcomes in patients with valvular heart disease (VD) underscores the critical need for more reliable depth-of-anesthesia monitoring. This study sought to investigate propofol sensitivity and establish specific electroencephalography (EEG) biomarkers to guide safer anesthesia delivery in this high-risk population.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: In this prospective observational trial, 40 patients (20 VD vs. 20 non-cardiac controls) received Schnider model-guided propofol target-controlled infusion. Primary outcome was effect-site propofol concentration at loss of consciousness (CeLOC). Prefrontal EEG dynamics (spectral power, bicoherence, burst suppression) were analyzed.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: VD patients exhibited 34% lower CeLOC versus controls (2.8 vs. 4.25 µg/mL; p = 0.0001). EEG revealed distinct neurosignatures: elevated β and θ power (β: 3.16 vs. 1.68, p = 0.0303; θ: 1.21 vs. 0.88, p = 0.0166), higher spectral power ratio (SPR: 1.38 vs. 1.98; p = 0.022), and intensified burst suppression (ratio: 0.11 vs. 0.32; p < 0.0001). Bicoherence analysis demonstrated impaired cross-frequency coupling in β and δ bands (β: 40.95 vs. 34.75, p = 0.0253; δ:43.5 vs. 30.95, p < 0.0001).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: VD is associated with heightened propofol sensitivity and distinct EEG signatures, including patterns suggestive of thalamocortical dysrhythmia and cortical suppression. These findings suggest that disease-specific EEG monitoring may inform the development of precision anesthesia protocols for cardiac populations.

funding-source.institution: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province
Outstanding Scientific Research and Innovation Team Program for Universities in Anhui Province

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Background
sec.p: General anesthesia is essential for cardiac valve surgery, requiring precise induction of reversible unconsciousness to optimize surgical conditions while ensuring patient safety. Anesthetic management in this population poses unique challenges: valvular pathologies impose excessive pressure and volume loads upon the heart, triggering ventricular remodeling and compensatory cardiac output adaptations [1]. These pathophysiological alterations critically compromise baseline hemodynamic stability and adversely impact central neurological function through the heart-brain axis pathway [2–4]. Patients undergoing cardiac surgery are at significantly elevated risk for several perioperative complications. This susceptibility stems from their unique pathophysiology and often necessitates modified anesthetic management, which together can lead to: exacerbated hemodynamic fluctuations due to impaired ventricular function and altered pharmacodynamics [5–7]; an increased risk of intraoperative awareness when anesthetic dosing is hemodynamically restricted [8, 9]; delayed emergence from the cumulative effects of sedatives administered with caution [10]; postoperative delirium associated with underlying cerebral hypoperfusion and a heightened systemic inflammatory response [11–13]; and a greater predisposition to long-term neurocognitive deficits [14, 15]. We hypothesize that these risks may be linked to altered anesthetic sensitivity, given the known alterations in cerebral perfusion, neuroendocrine signaling, and neurostructural integrity in valvular heart disease patients [1–4]. Consequently, developing individualized anesthetic strategies targeting the circulatory-neurological compensatory vulnerability induced by valvulopathy is imperative—a critical step toward enhancing perioperative safety and long-term neurological outcomes.

Clinical evidence indicates that patients with heart failure frequently exhibit reduced cerebral perfusion, elevated stress hormone levels (e.g., cortisol), and systemic inflammation - all factors synergistically contributing to cerebral dysfunction [16, 17]. Neuroimaging studies further reveal significantly increased prevalence of gray matter atrophy and white matter microstructural abnormalities even in clinically stable heart failure cohorts [18]. Notably, valvular heart insufficiency constitutes an independent risk factor for functional MRI anomalies, with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) identified as the primary predictor of white matter lesions [4, 19]. Crucially, such valvular heart pathology may alter anesthetic sensitivity through modified cerebral functional states. Consistent with hemodynamic stability requirements, Olivier et al. recommend a target propofol concentration of 1.5–2 µg/mL using target-controlled infusion (TCI) during cardiac valve surgery [20]. This aligns with findings demonstrating a lower predicted effect-site concentration for loss of consciousness (CeLOC) with propofol in cardiac surgical patients (1.43 µg/mL) [21] compared to non-cardiac surgical populations (2.69 µg/ml) [18]. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that valvular heart dysfunction directly and indirectly compromises cerebral structure and function. Within this pathophysiological framework, the brain’s response to general anesthetics may be modified; however, agent-specific manifestations of unconsciousness induction and their underlying central neural mechanisms remain to be elucidated.

Perioperative electroencephalogram (EEG) monitoring provides a critical window into anesthetic depth by capturing propofol-induced transitions in brain state: from moderate sedation (marked by reduced occipital alpha with increased spindle, beta and gamma activity) to deep sedation (characterized by a global shift of theta, alpha, spindle, and beta activity toward prefrontal regions) and finally to burst-suppression, a pattern indicative of cortical-thalamic decoupling [22]. Key EEG biomarkers for quantifying hypnotic depth and propofol sensitivity include spectral power, which reflects the distribution of oscillatory energy across frequency bands; burst-suppression, whose ratio and duration serve as measures of cortical excitation-inhibition balance; and bicoherence, which quantifies quadratic phase coupling to reveal thalamocortical oscillatory coordination under GABAergic modulation [23–25]. Notably, propofol induces a dose-dependent alpha and delta-theta coupling pattern in bicoherence spectra [26–28]. Collectively, these biomarkers offer quantifiable, objective neurophysiological insights into large-scale network synchronization during anesthesia, forming a basis for individualized depth monitoring and sensitivity assessment.

This study was designed to investigate propofol sensitivity and establish specific EEG biomarkers to guide safer anesthesia delivery in patients undergoing cardiac valvular surgery. By analyzing prefrontal EEG dynamics—including spectral power, bicoherence, and burst suppression patterns—during propofol-induced loss of consciousness (LOC) in these patients versus non-cardiac disease controls, this research aims to provide neurophysiological insights for personalized anesthesia management in this high-risk surgical population.

sec.title: Study population
sec.p: This prospective observational study received ethical approval from the Institutional Review Board (Approval No.: First Affiliated Hospital of Anhui Medical University PJ2023-06-12) and was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. The study was registered with the Chinese Clinical Trial Registry (Registration No.: ChiCTR2300072275). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to enrolment. Eligible participants were stratified into two cohorts: patients with valvular heart disease (VD) undergoing elective valvular surgery; and non-cardiac patients (Con) undergoing elective non-cardiac surgery. A single-blind design ensured researchers performing post-hoc analyses remained masked to group assignments.

The cohort comprised patients scheduled for valve replacement/repair (VD group) and non-cardiac surgical patients (Con group), aged 30–65 years. Exclusion criteria included: body mass index (BMI) ≥ 30 kg/m² (obesity) or < 18.5 kg/m² (underweight), dementia, intellectual disability, neuropsychiatric disorders, history of cranial neurosurgery or cerebrovascular disease, hearing impairment, or significant communication barriers. Attrition criteria encompassed: voluntary withdrawal, protocol deviation, or incomplete data collection.

sec.title: Preparation for the induction of anesthesia
sec.p: Patients fasted for ≥ 8 h preoperatively and received no premedication. Frailty status was assessed using the Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS). Upon operating room arrival, standard monitoring was initiated, including invasive blood pressure, electrocardiography, end-tidal carbon dioxide, and pulse oximetry. Baseline vital signs and electroencephalography (EEG) were recorded concurrently.

Frontal EEG signals were acquired using a 4-channel brain function monitor (Masimo Corp., Irvine, CA, USA). Electrodes were placed according to the international 10–20 system. Recording electrodes were positioned at Fp1 (left mid-frontal), Fp2 (right mid-frontal), F7 (left anterior temporal) and F8 (right anterior temporal). The ground electrode was placed at the midline frontal pole (approximating Fpz), with the reference electrode located 1 cm superior along the sagittal plane. Skin preparation included degreasing with alcohol swabs and application of conductive gel to maintain impedance below 5 kΩ. Signals were digitized at a sampling rate of 178 Hz with a preamplifier bandwidth of 0.5–89 Hz. Real-time artifact detection algorithms identified electromyogenic interference (> 40 Hz) or motion artifacts. An electrophysiology technician performed concurrent visual inspection to ensure signal fidelity.

sec.title: Induction of anesthesia
sec.p: Midazolam 0.03 mg/kg IV 30 min before anesthesia induction for sedation. Propofol (Diprivan®, Fresenius Kabi, Brézins, France) was administered via target-controlled infusion using the Schnider effector compartment pharmacokinetic model (Base Primea® infusion system, Fresenius Kabi) [29]. The initial target Ce was set at 1.0 µg/mL. This was followed by stepwise titration in 0.3 µg/mL increments. After each dose adjustment, 1 min equilibration period was maintained to ensure effect-site concentration (Ce) stabilization at the current target level before proceeding to further increases. Titration was continued until the patient reached a state of unconsciousness, defined as the absence of visible limb motor response to standardized audio stimulation on 3 consecutive occasions while frontal EEG signals showed sustained stability (without significant fluctuations). The predicted Ce displayed on the infusion pump at this point was recorded as CeLOC. After LOC, sufentanil (0.5 mg/kg) and cisatracurium (2 mg/kg) were administered to facilitate tracheal intubation.

The Patient State Index (PSI), a clinically validated measure of anesthesia depth, is a dimensionless value ranging from 100 (fully awake) to 0 (deep anesthesia), where lower values indicate greater hypnotic effect [30, 31]. The 95% spectral edge frequency (SEF95), a processed EEG parameter, typically ranges between 0 and 30 Hz, with lower values corresponding to reduced responsiveness [32, 33]. During anesthesia maintenance, the predicted Ce was adjusted to ensure PSI remained within 25–50 and SEF95 within 10–15. Hemodynamic parameters including arterial blood pressure were continuously monitored with strict maintenance criteria (± 20% of baseline values). This threshold was chosen based on clinical convention for high-risk patients, a pathophysiological rationale specific to valvular heart disease (balancing perfusion and ventricular strain), and alignment with published intervention thresholds in perioperative studies [34–36]. Protocol-defined interventions (atropine for bradycardia < 50 bpm; phenylephrine/ephedrine for mean blood pressure decrease > 20% baseline) were implemented immediately upon hemodynamic instability. Concurrent electroencephalographic monitoring was performed throughout the induction phase to capture neural dynamics for post hoc analysis. Mean blood pressure (MBP), heart rate (HR), PSI, and effect-site propofol concentration (Ceprop) were recorded at the following time points: baseline (pre-induction), LOC, immediately post-intubation 5, 10, and 15 min post-intubation.

sec.title: Data collection and EEG preprocessing
sec.p: Raw EEG signals underwent standardized preprocessing using EEGLAB (v2021.1, SCCN, San Diego) with the following sequential procedures. A zero-phase linear finite impulse response filter (0.5–50 Hz, 60 dB/octave roll-off) was applied to attenuate low-frequency drift (< 0.5 Hz) and high-frequency noise (> 50 Hz) while preserving neural oscillations of interest. Artifact correction was implemented through a three-stage protocol: (1) Initial automated detection using EEGLAB (± 100 µV threshold); (2) Independent visual verification by two researchers (1s/frame sampling) targeting three specific artifact types: residual EMG artifacts (20–60 Hz), ocular artifacts (characterized by slow, large-amplitude deflections and synchronous deflections across frontal channels, Fp1/Fp2), as well as amplifier noise such as signal saturation, abrupt baseline shifts, or persistent 50 Hz line noise; (3) Quality control requiring an inter-rater reliability of > 0.8 (Cohen’s κ), with any discrepancies resolved by arbitration from senior electrophysiologists (> 10 years’ experience). EEG signals failing to meet the predefined technical criteria for interpretable signal quality—defined as insufficient for clinical interpretation—were excluded. For the included data, artifact removal was performed at the epoch level. Entire 2‑second epochs were removed if any channel exhibited a clear artifact (as defined above) lasting > 0.5 s. Only epochs that were free of obvious artifacts across all channels were retained. To ensure high signal quality, we employed a strict inclusion criterion: each subject’s EEG recording used for core analyses had to contain > 95% artifact‑free (clean) data.

Within the EEG recording protocol, t = 0 was defined as the onset of propofol infusion, t = LOC was defined as the time of LOC, determined by the onset of both behavioral unresponsiveness and sustained EEG stability and t = End was defined as 15 min after tracheal intubation. Analysis epochs spanned from 60 s before propofol injection (t = -60) to 60 s after the t = End time point. Critical phases included Baseline (t=-60s→0), LOC transition (LOC0→LOC + 60s) and End (End0→End + 60s). Data segments containing residual artifacts underwent consensus-based rejection using established criteria [37], retaining only epochs with > 95% artifact-free signals. Processed data were analyzed using three methods including spectral power distribution, biphasic coherence analysis and burst suppression quantification. These analyses were implemented using custom MATLAB (R2022a, MathWorks) scripts.

Power spectra (quantifying EEG power distribution across frequencies) and spectrograms (time-resolved spectral representations) were generated through multitaper spectral analysis using 2-second sliding windows with zero overlap, a time-bandwidth product of 3, 5 tapers, and 3 Hz spectral resolution. Group-level analysis involved temporal alignment of target timepoints across subjects, followed by median aggregation of power spectra. Bootstrap resampling (10,000 iterations) with percentile-derived 95% confidence intervals was applied to quantify statistical uncertainty. Significant inter-timepoint differences required dual criteria: (1) contiguous frequency bands exceeding the 3 Hz spectral resolution bandwidth, and (2) non-zero-inclusive confidence intervals. Canonical frequency bands (δ: 1–4 Hz, θ: 4–8 Hz, α: 8–13 Hz, β: 13–30 Hz, γ: 30–45 Hz) were identified using an automated detection pipeline. For each canonical frequency band, the peak power (the maximum power value within the band) was extracted and used in all subsequent band‑specific analyses. SPR was calculated as (δ + θ)/(α + β), where higher values indicate deeper anesthetic states. The peak power is the highest power within canonical frequency bands [38].

Bicoherence is a normalized bispectral measure that quantifies quadratic phase coupling between frequency components within a signal, independent of signal power. This nonlinear analysis detects whether the phase of an oscillation at a frequency f1 + f2 is consistently the sum of the phases at f1 and f2, revealing a specific form of phase synchronization that indicates nonlinear neural interactions. Crucially, high bicoherence values are theorized to reflect stable, reverberant activity within thalamocortical networks—a key neural substrate for conscious states [39, 40]. As such, bicoherence can capture the integrity of this re-entrant system even from a single EEG channel (e.g., over the prefrontal cortex), providing a unique window into brain-state dynamics that may not be evident in the power spectrum or linear coherence between regions [23, 25]. In this study, we employed a validated, standardized bispectral analysis method to assess the nonlinear phase coupling in the EEG signal, thereby evaluating the synchronization of neural oscillations within thalamocortical circuits. The detailed methodology follows below.
where:
Subsequently, the normalized bicoherence value, expressed as a percentage, was calculated:

Burst suppression parameters were manually quantified from t = 0 to t = End. Bursts were defined as episodes > 0.5 s with voltage excursions exceeding 5 µV, suppressions were defined as a period > 0.5 s with peak-to-peak amplitude ± 5 µV [41, 42]. Transition points were defined as instantaneous amplitude crossings of the 5 µV threshold. Burst suppression ratio was calculated as: (Total suppression duration/Epoch length) × 100%.

sec.title: Statistical analysis of the data
sec.p: The primary endpoint was the propofol CeLOC. The sample size was calculated based on data from a dedicated, unpublished pilot study. The pilot measured CeLOC values of (2.94 ± 0.25) µg/ml in VD patients and (3.64 ± 1.02) µg/ml in healthy controls. With these parameters (α = 0.05, two-tailed; power = 80%), the minimum sample size calculated using PASS software was 18 per group. To account for an anticipated 10% dropout rate, enrollment was expanded to 25 participants per cohort.

Continuous variables were assessed for normality using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test with Lilliefors correction. For normally distributed data, values are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Inter-group comparisons were analyzed using two-tailed independent Student’s t-tests, and 95% confidence intervals (95% CI) for mean differences were calculated. For comparisons over time between the two groups, a two-way repeated-measures analysis of variance (RM ANOVA) was performed, followed by Bonferroni post hoc analysis. For non-normally distributed data, data are expressed as median (interquartile range, IQR). Group comparisons were performed using Mann-Whitney U tests, and 95% CI for group differences were calculated using the Hodges-Lehmann estimator and its associated confidence interval method. Categorical variables were summarized as absolute frequencies with percentages [n (%)], with between-group differences evaluated through Pearson’s χ² test or Fisher’s exact test (applied when expected counts < 5). All analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics (v24.0, Armonk, NY) with graphical representations generated in GraphPad Prism (v8.0.1, San Diego, CA). Statistical significance was determined using two-sided tests with p < 0.05 as the cutoff criterion.

sec.title: Baseline characteristics of the patients
sec.p: Figure 1 illustrates the participant flow according to STROBE(strobe-statement.org)guidelines. From 50 individuals assessed for eligibility, 10 were excluded: 4 failed to meet inclusion criteria and 3 declined participation (3 exclusions unspecified). The remaining 43 enrolled participants were allocated to either the Valvular Heart Disease (VD) Group (n = 22) or non-cardiac Disease (Con) Group (n = 21). Pre-analysis exclusions occurred due to uninterpretable EEG recordings (VD Group: n = 2; Con Group: n = 1). Consequently, 20 participants per group were included in the final analysis, yielding balanced cohorts of 40 total analyzed subjects.

caption.p: CONSORT Participant Flow Diagram: Screening, Enrollment, and Allocation in the Trial

sec.p: There were no significant differences in baseline parameters including age, sex, and body mass index (BMI) between the two groups. Valvular heart disease patients exhibited varying degrees of impaired cardiac function, with ejection fraction ranging from 40% to 68% and a median value of 60.5%. Compared to the control group, patients with valvular heart disease had significantly higher scores on the American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA) physical status classification, New York Heart Association (NYHA) functional classification, Clinical Frailty Scale (CFS). Valve replacement surgery was performed in VD patients, while Con patients underwent various non-cardiac procedures, including gastrointestinal surgery (4 cases), orthopedic surgery (8 cases), spine surgery (2 cases), breast surgery (4 cases), and ear, nose, and throat surgery (2 cases) (Table 1).

table-wrap-foot.p: Values are presented as no. (%), mean ± SD or median (inter-quartile range [IQR])

Abbreviations: VD valvular heart disease, CI confidence interval, BMI body mass index, LBW lean body weight, ASA American Society of Anesthesiologists, NYHA New York Heart Association. CFS Clinical Frailty Scale

sec.title: Disease-specific differences in propofol response: enhanced sensitivity in valvular heart disease patients
sec.p: During anesthesia, the Ceprop in VD patients was significantly lower than that in the controls at multiple time points, including LOC, intubation, and 5 min post-intubation (Table 2). Notably, the primary outcome (CeLOC defined as Ceprop at loss of consciousness) was 34% lower in VD patients than in controls (2.8 µg/mL [IQR 2.5–3.1] vs. 4.25 µg/mL [IQR 3.2–4.58]; p = 0.0001; 95% CI, -1.7 to -0.6). Following combined with sufentanil for intubation, Ceprop decreased further in both groups. Compared to the controls, VD patients showed greater reductions in Ceprop at intubation (2.2 µg/mL [IQR: 1.83-3] vs. 3.15 µg/mL [IQR: 2.8–4.58]; p = 0.001, 95% CI: -2 to -0.3), 5 min post-intubation (2.22 ± 0.9 µg/mL vs. 3.45 ± 1.41 µg/mL; p = 0.002, 95% CI: -1.98 to -0.47), 10 min post-intubation (3.05 ± 1.33 µg/mL vs. 2.23 ± 0.87 µg/mL; p = 0.025, 95% CI: -1.54 to -0.11) and 15 min post-intubation (2.88 ± 1.2 µg/mL vs. 2.12 ± 0.84 µg/mL; p = 0.026, 95% CI: -1.42 to -0.09). These results indicate that valvular heart disease enhances sensitivity to propofol, and opioid coadministration further potentiates this effect.

caption.p: Valvular heart disease required lower Ceprop compared with controls

table-wrap-foot.p: Values are presented as no. (%), mean ± SD or median (inter-quartile range [IQR])

sec.title: Vital signs and anesthetic depth dynamics during anesthesia
sec.p: No significant difference in MBP was observed between the two groups (F (1, 38) = 0.0033, p = 0.955, 95% CI: -4.87 to 5.15) (Table 3). Compared with controls, VD patients required higher doses of phenylephrine to maintain hemodynamic stability (200 ug (100–300) vs. 0 ug (0–0); p < 0.0001; 95% CI: 160–260) (Table 3). Notably, patients with valvular heart disease exhibited preoperative tachycardia compared to controls, and this significant difference persisted until intubation, suggesting a compensatory response to cardiac dysfunction (F (1, 38) = 8.246, p = 0.007, 95% CI: -25.72 to -4.45)) (Table 3). Furthermore, no intergroup differences in anesthetic depth were detected by the PSI (F (1, 38) = 1.29, p = 0.263, 95% CI: -1.9 to 6.77) (Table 3).

caption.p: Monitoring of vital signs and anesthetic depth dynamics

table-wrap-foot.p: Abbreviations: Patient State Index, PSI. Mean blood pressure. Heart rate and PSI were analyzed using two-way RM ANOVA

sec.title: EEG spectral analysis in the propofol-induced unconsciousness phase
sec.p: Spectral analysis revealed comparable EEG power distributions between groups during the pre-induction phase (Figs. 2). However, distinct patterns reflecting pathological differences emerged during propofol-induced LOC. The VD group exhibited significantly elevated β-wave power (3.16 ± 2.67 vs. 1.68 ± 0.11, p = 0.0303, 95% CI: 0.13 to 2.83) and θ-wave power (1.21 ± 0.32 vs. 0.88 ± 0.49, p = 0.0166, 95% CI: 0.06 to 0.59) versus controls. No significant intergroup differences were observed in other clinically significant frequency bands (α, δ, and γ) (Table 4).

caption.p: Analysis of group-level power spectra during propofol-induced unconsciousness

sec.title: SPR during propofol-induced unconsciousness SPR during
sec.p: The SPR (δ + θ/α + β) provides anesthetic-induced modulation of brain activity by integrating measures of slow rhythms and fast oscillations [43]. No significant between-group difference was observed in baseline SPR profiles (1.88 [1.56–2.61] vs. 1.81 [1.37–2.2]; p = 0.433, 95% CI: -0.55 to 2.5) (Table 5). Notably, compared with controls, propofol administration induced a significantly higher SPR at LOC in VD patients (1.38 ± 0.7 vs. 1.98 ± 0.88; p = 0.022, 95% CI: 0.09 to 1.11) (Table 5)

caption.p: Valvular heart disease patients exhibited increased SPR at LOC

sec.title: Biphasic dynamics of pathologic differences in propofol-induced unconsciousness
sec.p: Bispectral phase coupling analysis characterizes nonlinear cortico-cortical communication dynamics through detecting phase-synchronized interactions among triple frequency combinations. Our bidirectional connectivity mapping revealed pathological alterations in network reorganization patterns during propofol-induced hypnosis (Fig. 3). Compared with controls, the VD group exhibited significantly reduced power in the beta (40.95 ± 9.25 vs. 34.75 ± 7.8, p = 0.0253, 95% CI: -11.59 to -0.81) (Table 6) and delta (43.5 ± 7.19 vs. 30.95 ± 5.59, p < 0.0001, 95% CI: -16.67 to -8.43) (Table 6) frequency bands, while no significant differences were observed in other frequency bands (Table 6)

caption.p: EEG spectral characterization of neural dynamics associated with pathologic differences. (A) Consciousness transition trajectory: prefrontal spectral power dynamics of pathological differences during propofol induction. (B) Comparison of group-level power spectra during propofol-induced unconsciousness. The color scale range in the spectrogram typically indicates relative power intensity in decibels (dB)
Comparative analysis of spectral power peaks in cross-frequency EEG bands

sec.title: EEG burst suppression patterns during propofol anesthesia
sec.p: Compared with controls, the VD group exhibited significantly elevated burst suppression metrics during propofol anesthesia (Fig. 2), including burst suppression ratio (0.11 [0.06–0.16] vs. 0.32 [0.21–0.41], p < 0.0001, 95% CI: 0.130 to 0.28), percent suppression time (0.43 ± 0.04 vs. 0.54 ± 0.08, p < 0.0001, 95% CI: 0.69 to 0.17), maximum suppression epoch duration (1.23 ± 0.43 vs. 2.09 ± 0.61, p < 0.0001, 95% CI: 0.51 to 1.22) and state transition frequency between burst/suppression phases (20.75 ± 13.49 vs. 48.75 ± 21.27, p < 0.0001, 95% CI: 16.22 to 39.8) (Table 7). These findings indicate profound and sustained cortical suppression.

caption.p: EEG bicoherence patterns at propofol-induced LOC. (A) Bicoherence spectral profiles demonstrating two groups’ cross-frequency coupling characteristics. (B) Diagonal bicoherence components revealing fundamental frequency interactions in both groups
VD group exhibited significantly higher values in burst suppression patterns

tr.td: Number of transitions into and out of burst suppression

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study provides a systematic assessment of propofol anesthetic sensitivity and its associated EEG signatures in valvular heart disease patients. We demonstrate that VD patients exhibit significantly lower Ceprop at LOC, with this hypersensitivity persisting until 5 min post-intubation. This enhanced sensitivity manifested distinct EEG signatures: elevated β and θ-band spectral power, increased SPR, and abnormally heightened bicoherence. Moreover, significantly intensified burst suppression patterns were observed during propofol anesthesia. These findings provide neurophysiological evidence for a unique propofol hypersensitivity profile in VD, offering critical insights into potential neural alterations associated with anesthetic sensitivity within valvular pathophysiology.

The enhanced δ-band power observed during LOC is a well-established electrophysiological marker of profound cortical inhibition under GABAergic anesthesia. In such states, suppressed thalamic relay function reduces ascending sensory input and promotes large-scale cortical synchronization, leading to dominant slow-wave activity [44]. This pattern is therefore widely interpreted in the literature as reflecting a functional decoupling or compromised integrity within the thalamocortical network [45, 46]. In the context of VD, chronic cerebral hypoperfusion and neurostructural changes may predispose this network to enter such a synchronized state more readily [47]. This aligns with the elevated spectral power ratio, indicative of pathological shifts toward inhibitory-dominant brain states. The abnormally increased bicoherence indicates excessive phase-coupling between frequency components, which may reflect disrupted cross-regional communication and reduced neural complexity - features commonly associated with impaired consciousness maintenance [25, 27]. Crucially, the intensified burst suppression is indicative of a heightened cortical excitability-inhibition imbalance [33, 41]. The burst-suppression pattern itself is an emergent network state characterized by alternating epochs of synchronous neuronal depolarization (“bursts”) and widespread hyperpolarization (“suppressions”). This alternation is a hallmark of a profoundly disrupted E-I balance at the cortical network level. Within this pattern, prolonged suppression epochs may correspond to periods of profound neural inertia [48], while frequent state transitions could imply instability in network resilience [27, 48]. Collectively, these signatures characterize a brain state exhibiting heightened vulnerability to anesthetic suppression. This vulnerability may arise within the context of the neurostructural and functional alterations associated with valvular heart disease [49, 50], which are known to influence network-level responses to GABAergic agents like propofol [47, 48].

The synergistic phenomenon in VD patients—where LOC occurs at a lower propofol concentration and coincides with intensified EEG suppression—likely stems from VD-specific neuropathological remodeling, which predisposes the cortical network to enter a state of profound suppression with reduced anesthetic challenge. Neuroendocrine dysregulation serves as a critical pathophysiological conduit: cardiac-derived hormones traversing the compromised blood-brain barrier [51], coupled with chronic hypoperfusion and inflammation-induced neuronal damage [52], trigger compensatory upregulation of GABA A receptors. Concomitant hypoxia-driven synaptic pruning abnormalities reduce neural network connectivity and impair signal integration capacity [53, 54]. This heart disease-induced neural fragility explains the dual manifestations: heightened sensitivity to low-dose propofol and impaired maintenance of stable electrical activity—two facets of the same neuropathological process.

Conventional neuromonitors such as the PSI exhibit significant limitations in valvular heart disease patients. Developed based on general population models, these tools often fail to accurately capture the distinct cortical oscillatory patterns caused by impaired cerebral perfusion and disease-specific neurovascular coupling [16, 19, 55, 57]. This critical shortcoming underscores the urgent need to transition toward a multi-parameter EEG framework in cardiac anesthesia—one that integrates dynamic β and θ power, SPR, bicoherence phase mapping, and automated burst suppression ratio quantification. Although the observed intergroup EEG differences may be subtle, they highlight the necessity of exploring disease-specific EEG dynamics and developing novel biomarkers aligned with the unique physiology of VD patients. Ultimately, clinical guidance should incorporate these neurophysiological biomarkers into personalized precision protocols that also consider real-time hemodynamics, pharmacological profiles, surgical stimulus intensity, and the anesthesiologist’s comprehensive judgment.

Patients with valvular heart disease required significantly higher propofol CeLOC (Schnider model: 2.8 µg/ml) compared to literature values reported for general cardiac patients (Marsh model: 1.43 µg/ml) [21]. This discrepancy stems from the Schnider model’s unique pharmacokinetic profile, which features a smaller central volume of distribution and a steeper initial peak concentration. These characteristics are further amplified by the use of an ultra-gradual infusion gradient, prolonging both the induction and redistribution phases—a particular concern in valvular heart disease that likely contributes to the divergence between predicted and actual propofol distribution.

To ensure consistency and minimize variability, a strict protocol was followed: both the initial target concentration and subsequent stepwise increases during TCI were set low, with sufficient time allowed after each step to achieve equilibrium between plasma and effect-site concentrations. The determination of LOC was rigorously standardized and assessed by a single investigator using uniform verbal commands and observational criteria to eliminate inter-observer variability. This approach enhances the objectivity and reproducibility of CeLOC as an endpoint within pharmacokinetic model-based administration. Future studies should prioritize direct measurement of plasma propofol concentrations to validate pharmacokinetic model predictions in this patient population. Additionally, cross-validation with other models, such as the cardiac output-informed Eleveld model, may provide further mechanistic insight.

This investigation has several noteworthy constraints. First, limited generalizability beyond specific valvular pathologies arises from substantial pathophysiological heterogeneity across cardiac diseases, warranting future multicenter validation. Second, inadequate sample size prevents comprehensive subgroup stratification—particularly regarding valvulopathy subtypes and severity gradations. Third, the clinical translatability of observed neurostructural remodeling patterns—which alter propofol sensitivity and oscillatory network dynamics—remains undetermined for other anesthetic agents, given fundamental pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic divergences. Fourth, the electroencephalographic bicoherence analysis under our parameters principally reflects the temporal consistency of oscillations, although it has been conventionally used in anesthetic neurophysiology to assess network coupling [23, 25, 39, 40]. Future studies could employ more direct measures of temporal consistency for further validation.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Valvular heart disease patients demonstrate heightened propofol sensitivity with distinct neural oscillatory alterations. This provides neurophysiological evidence explaining differential anesthetic responses and advances theoretical frameworks for consciousness modulation in pathological states. Future studies should integrate multimodal neuroimaging to decipher disease heterogeneity-driven network dynamics, refining personalized anesthesia through integrated neuro-endocrine-metabolic profiling.

def.p: effect-site concentration for loss of consciousness

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.
Yu Mao, Ying Shi and Liying Zhu contributed equally to this work.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Yu Mao, Ying Shi and Liying Zhu contributed equally to this work. Conceptualization: Y.M. and L.Z.; Methodology: Y.M., L.Z., and Y.S.; Software: X.S. and X.D.; Validation: Q.W., Z.H., and J.H.; Formal Analysis: Y.M. and L.Z.; Investigation: Y.M., L.Z., Y.S., Q.W., and Z.H.; Resources: E.G., G.X., and Y.J.; Data Curation: X.S. and L.Z.; Writing - Original Draft Preparation: Y.M. and L.Z.; Writing - Review & Editing: All authors; Visualization: X.D. and J.H.; Supervision: L.Z., E.G., and G.X.; Project Administration: Y.M. and Y.J.; Funding Acquisition: Y.M., X.S. and G.X.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants 82471285, 82401441, 82101301), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (2308085Y24) and Outstanding Scientific Research and Innovation Team Program for Universities in Anhui Province (2024AH010015).

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets used and/or analysed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the First Affiliated Hospital of Anhui Medical University (Approval No. ANMU LunShen-PJ PJ2023-06-12). The trial was registered prospectively with the China Clinical Trial Registry (ChiCTR2300072275).

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
